title: Honey-roast carrots and parsnips
subtitle: Both vegetables, glazed together in honey and butter
excerpt: Carrots and parsnips par-boiled and roasted together in butter and honey until sticky, golden, and sweet. The classic combined side for Christmas and Sunday lunch.
sourceNotes: Classic British side dish pairing. Both vegetables appear on the Christmas table and the combination became standard in British cooking from the 1980s.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Par-boil both vegetables up to 4 hours ahead. Store separately until roasting.

glossaryTerms.definition: Partially cooking vegetables in boiling salted water before roasting. Ensures the dense root vegetables are nearly tender before the oven finishes them.
The darkening and sweetening of the natural sugars in the vegetable as it roasts in honey and butter, producing the characteristic sticky, slightly bitter edges.

content.text: Carrots and parsnips cook at different rates in a hot oven. Par-boiling both together for 6 minutes levels this out. By the time they are roasting, both are close to tender. They can then be glazed together with honey for the final 10 minutes without either burning or undercooking.

items.prepNote: peeled, halved lengthways, cored if large

content.text: Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan.
the carrots and parsnips together in well-salted boiling water for 6 minutes. Drain well and leave to steam-dry in the colander.
Heat the butter and oil in the roasting tin for 3 minutes until the butter foams. Add the vegetables and turn to coat. Add the thyme. Season with salt. Roast for 25 minutes, turning once.
Drizzle the honey over the vegetables. Return to the oven for a further 10 minutes until

items.symptom: The honey has burnt before the vegetables are glazed
items.cause: Honey was added too early in the cooking.
items.fix: Add honey only in the last 10 minutes. It will darken quickly.

items.symptom: The parsnips are golden but the carrots are still firm
items.cause: The carrot pieces were thicker than the parsnip pieces.
items.fix: Cut both vegetables to similar diameters: no thicker than 2 cm at the thickest point. Parsnips often need to be quartered and cored if large.

items.symptom: The vegetables are cooked but pale, without the deep caramel colour
items.cause: Too much moisture remaining from the par-boil, or the tin was overcrowded.
items.fix: Shake the colander vigorously to remove all steam before roasting. Give the vegetables plenty of space in a wide tin.

items.symptom: The honey glaze has crystallised and the vegetables are sticky but not caramelised
items.cause: The oven temperature dropped during roasting.
items.fix: Maintain a steady 200 degrees C fan. The glaze needs sustained heat to caramelise rather than just set.

content.text: Add a teaspoon of wholegrain mustard to the honey for a mustard glaze. A pinch of cumin or coriander in the butter gives a warm spiced background. Maple syrup is a slightly more complex alternative to honey.

Honey-roast vegetables became a fixture on British Christmas tables in the 1980s. Food writers began recommending vegetable sides that could match the richness of the main course. The combination of carrots and parsnips is now so embedded in the British Christmas tradition that it appears on most supermarket Christmas side dish ranges.
